segments.text: Just don't try and have any sister acts.
Yes, car.
Good morning.
Good morning.
Yeah.
I didn't get a walk this morning because of the work.
Oh, I'm so sorry.
Me neither.
Hopefully you can do it afterwards, get a bit of fresh air, clear your mind.
Welcome ministers, welcome officials.
That you all get settled in.
Good morning. We're just getting ourselves settled.
Yes. It's okay.
Is that all right?
If someone wants to shut this door, that would be
good.
No.
All right then. We are here to hear from both the minister and associate
minister for ACC on ACC as part of our vote
labor
budget estimates. We have about an hour 10
with you, so that'd be typically around about sort of three and a half,
four minutes for opening remarks.
We'll leave that to you, and then we'll open for questions.
Yeah. Thank you, Madam Chair, and good morning, members, at this hour.
We appreciate the commitment of the committee to meet at 8:00 AM.
I have got some opening comments, and I've also asked the associate minister to
make some comments as well. So I'll just go quickly through it.
So,
ACC is a foundational
institution in New Zealand society.
It's a really important aspect of what it is to be a Kiwi,
and I feel very privileged to have the responsibility and the privilege of
being minister of ACC and supported so ably by the Associate Minister
Greg. But ACC's been serving our communities
for 50 years now.
Over those period of years, it's expanded for a number of reasons,
usually quite good reasons.
One of the first things that I did upon becoming minister was to go
back and read the original Sir Owen Woodhouse Royal Commission report that
established ACC. It was a report written in
1967. The language is language, in some
cases, we wouldn't use today. But ACC was quite narrow in
its definition.
But over the years, it has been expanded, often for
societal reasons, because attitudes and society
has changed over those 50 years. So I want to provide a little bit of an update
on what progress we've been making over the last 12 months.
But before I do, I want to introduce our board chair, Jan Dawson.
Jan is the new board chair. Members who were on this committee last
year will remember that we had the acting board chair, David Hunt, here
last year. But Jan's been in the role now for what? Seven months.
About that sort of time.
Yeah.
Megan Mayne, chief executive. And we've got other officials
in the room, including Nick Blakely from MB.
So I want to just talk about my priorities for ACC.
It is a uniquely Kiwi scheme. There is nothing quite like ACC
in any other jurisdiction in the world.
And I think we can, as New Zealanders, be proud of that.
Part of the reason it works so well is because we have a relatively flat
kind of legislative model. We don't have a federal system.
We don't have states. We don't have competing
entities or
whatever. And it is a no litigation, no
fault scheme. We don't talk about that enough these days, in my view, but I think
it's important. But over the years, ACC has
actually become a very large beast.
The numbers involved are eye-wateringly large, and no doubt we'll get a
chance to talk about some of those as we go through this hearing.
So in terms of my priorities, I want to ensure that
ACC is on a stable footing and will be
secure and sustainable for the next 50 years and beyond.
But if we aren't careful, that won't be the case.
There are a number of hurdles and challenges ahead of ACC.
But I'm pleased to say that the board is working on a turnaround plan,
and we'll have a chance to talk about that shortly.
But my priorities are essentially putting clients first.
And at its heart, ACC is about people.
It's about individuals, about people.
And if we get the people part right at ACC, then all the
rest flows from that and kind of sorts itself
out. So putting clients first with the right care and support that they
need and deserve is important to me.
And then secondly, helping New Zealanders return to what
ACC refers to as independence. Getting them back to work,
getting them back to being a functioning member of
society once they have had their
injury
rehabilitated and sorted out. And then
my third
priority is actually getting the organization back to basics.
There was a report that was published last year. Finity did a report.
Some of you will have read that report.
And essentially what it said was that ACC needed to get back to
basics, doing the- Things that they are mandated to do, legislated to do,
day in, day out, consistently, reliably, efficiently, effectively,
and the rest follows. So,
that operational performance had declined over the last 10 years or so.
That turnaround plan I've talked about addresses those matters.
We're in the early stages of that turnaround plan, but we are starting to see
already, I think, some positive results that provide better
outcomes for New Zealanders.
There have been a number of court cases that have expanded the
boundaries of the scheme. But I
wanted to make it clear at the outset that whilst
those decisions have increased the costs of the scheme substantially,
the government is not currently considering any significant changes
to scheme boundaries.
I'm actively involved in updating and focused on some of the
regulatory aspects of ACC, and we can talk about more of that later.
But injury prevention is a big part of the scheme.
Obviously, don't have an accident in the first place helps the scheme work.
And so earlier,
in fact, it was late last year actually, I gave
delegated authority for injury prevention for ACC to the
associate minister. I'm going to ask Nicola Gregg now to make a couple of comments
and speak about a report that will be published very shortly this morning.
Thank you, Minister. Morning, everyone.
Thank you very much for this opportunity.
We're looking forward to speaking about this brilliant entity.
Look, I want to speak to some of the progress that ACC has made in
recent times, preventing injuries and, of course, those
challenges are ongoing and going forward.
But I think it goes without saying that reducing the
incidences and severity of
injuries is our core function.
And we've got to be able to work in a way that eases the financial strain
of the scheme. So you will all have, in your hands,
this just-released report that I want to talk to in the context
of my injury prevention delegation.
This is a snapshot of the 2025 injuries
in New Zealand,
and it will be publicly released later today.
So you really do have it hot off the press.
But
granted you've not had enough time to go through it, but just quickly,
it highlights the impact that injuries are having on the New
Zealand economy. You will see the stand out figure is
$8.7 billion in lost productivity.
Sorry to interrupt you. We've actually reached the limit of our
introductory remarks.
Oh, I had so much more to say. But we could-
But we've got lots of questions to ask you, Minister.
So we'll come back to that perhaps-
By all means
... as a question, if that's okay.
By all means.
I have opening question from Lawrence Yuen, and then we'll come back to you on
injury, because I have a question for that, and looks like Karl does, too.
And
yep, brilliant.
Thank you.
We've got it all stacked up, haven't we?
Thank you, Chair. I guess we definitely do want to discuss more on injury
prevention, so there'll definitely be a chance for the minister.
My opening question is around what we're looking at forecast in
terms of
the budget for ACC this year, and one of the things intend
them to some of the other works being done in other
areas. So, for example,
you would have undoubtedly seen now that it's released,
the department, sorry, not the department, the Select Committee report on the
Health and Safety at Work Bill, where ACC has addressed some serious concerns
around the cost that this will create.
Can you just confirm to the committee
what would be that cost for the forecast with the changes to the Health and
Safety at Work Act?
Sorry, can-
As well as the claims limit
... sorry, can you-- Lawrence, can you just explain again, because you said it's
the health-
No one will have seen that report because it's not been-
The other select-
... reported to the House yet.
No, I'm just at a loss as to quite what your question is.
Well, it should be this afternoon.
This afternoon.
We are on Wednesday, yes.
So
because I want to check with the minister,
whether the minister would agree with ACC officials that a Health and Safety at
Work Amendment Bill changes
to the limit duties on small businesses to critical risk
are unnecessary due to the current act is generally fit for
purpose, and changes are likely to lead to harm, loss of productivity,
and greater costs to the scheme. And if
so, what budget has been factored into
ACC's budget for this coming year to address the changes with
Health and Safety at Work?
Okay.
Well, what I would say is it's a bit hypothetical at this stage.
That legislation hasn't been passed.
ACC, as I said, is a no-fault, no litigation scheme.
So the scheme responds to the
environment in which it operates.
And there's an operational environment that the scheme doesn't
control the boundary. But I'm going to ask Megan to maybe make a
comment.
Morning, Madam Chair, committee.
So in the agency consultation, we shared some data
around the cost of injuries in workplaces.
Mm-hmm.
And
we expressed some data around what
we see as what we call the less serious injuries, so the
sprains and strains, those sorts of injuries that happen in the workplace.
I think the point I would like to make is
keeping people safe, whether it's at work or on the road, is everybody's
responsibility. So that's why our focus is
individuals, employers, whanau, community, making
sure everyone's playing their part.
And the second role for us is using our
data to make sure that where we're active in injury prevention,
it's where we see the highest impact on claim costs.
So we tend to focus on the areas with the highest claim costs
overall. So in workplaces, that's
manufacturing, forestry,
agriculture, construction, and we've seen some really positive
results where we focus with the experts, like the
Forestry Industry Safety Council,
for a long period of time. So for us, injury prevention in workplaces
is a long-term game.
I guess, yeah, just to follow up a question, and this ties into what the minister
was going to say in terms of injury prevention, is what changes would
ACC need to make in terms of the way it discusses workplace
injury prevention, particularly for what you mentioned, the
musculoskeletal harms that could happen for small
businesses now they're no longer re-- Well, they would be no longer
required to manage those risks.
So we would continue to focus on the same messages.
Have a hmm, do everything you can to keep yourself and your
workplace safe. So that doesn't change.
And the second thing, particularly around musculoskeletal injuries, is
when someone is injured, that our recent messaging has been to be
really active in your recovery. And that includes doing all your rehab,
working with your health provider, but also getting back to work
where possible, as early as possible.
So one of the areas we're really focused on with employers is recovery
at work and helping employers to understand that
just being back in the workplace, not necessarily doing your job, because
we'd still be paying weekly compensation to the person, but being back,
that social connection to your work mates helps recovery.
And there's plenty of international evidence that that's good for recovery.
So that's certainly our area of interest in terms
of workplace harm. It's both preventing it and minimizing
the long-term impacts.
Just one final step on that,
Chair.
But the issue that
would appear is that because workplace is no longer required to
manage those kind of risks,
how are you going to be able to track some of the data around that?
It's all very well what
you're saying in terms of overseas examples, but also other overseas
jurisdictions have better work safety protections for
workers, particularly for small businesses when it comes to musculoskeletal harm.
So I've got both my ministers here, they can talk to
that. At the moment, there's been no change-
No, exactly. As I say, it's currently hypothetical
... to the legislation. And we collect data on all injuries.
So over time, we see all sorts of trends in injuries
across the spectrum.
Thank you.
Okay. We have a supplementary from Priyanka Radhakrishnan.
Thank you. With respect, this is a political decision, Minister, the questioning
that, or the questions that Lawrence has asked, because the
legislation that will pass, because you have the numbers to pass them,
will create a small PCBU category for
companies with fewer than 20 employees.
Now, these firms will only be required to
manage critical risks, so death,
notifiable injury, occupational disease.
So that excludes the musculoskeletal harm that Lawrence was
talking about, psychosocial harms as well.
The policy logic there assumes that smaller firms pose
lower risks. So in light of what you'd have said today,
where ACC's focus is on injury prevention within the workplace,
I've tested this against ACC's own data, which
clearly shows that the claim
gradient runs in the opposite direction to what the bill assumes.
So the smallest firms with one to five workers have the highest rate
of injury at 73 per 1,000, compared to 53 per
1,000 for larger firms of over 500 employees.
So the political decision that your government's making on one side of the
spectrum with the Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill,
will lead to an increase in injury at the workplace, which
ACC is going to have to deal with, with no budget increase.
So how do you propose
to deal with that? What evidence or what advice have you received?
And
why are you making this political move?
Well, I'm not the Minister for Workplace Relations and Safety.
The piece of-
Part of government though
... the piece of policy,
legislative initiative that you're referring to is the responsibility of another
minister, and those questions are better asked of
the responsible minister, not me.
So is that not a conversation you've had with your colleagues?
Well, there is nothing
yet passed. The legislation is before a select committee, as I
understand it.
It's just been
deliberated.
Just been deliberated.
And so it's got to come back to the House.
ACC is an entity that
responds to situations when they become an eventuality.
At this stage, this is entirely hypothetical.
And whilst I understand the
path of the questioning, this is not currently a matter for ACC.
Okay. Our next question is for Carl Bates.
Thanks very much, Chair. In looking at the way ACC works
with other government agencies, if we change tack a bit, just
interested to understand how ACC is focused
on working with other agencies to deliver better services,
for example, ambulance and the like.
Yeah. Well,
ACC has a range
of
relationships with other agencies, other ministries, and one of
the
factors that came out of the budget was
some improved funding for
ambulance support. Another area, for instance, is in rescue
helicopters. We do a lot of that. And so
those are areas where ACC is tasked with
working with other agencies, other government departments, to create
better results in terms of access, but also
quickness and pace. So those are, I think, areas that we can
be quite pleased with. There is a-
A relatively collegial, well, it's a very collegial approach between
government agencies and departments.
I don't know whether anyone else might want to comment further.
Well, we have been working with Pharmac as well.
Mm-hmm, Pharmac.
And also the Inland Revenue. So working cross agencies-
Mm
... is really important to be effective and efficient in our
role.
And, thanks, Chair. Do you use any tools?
Are you looking to use AI in any way to find some of those efficiencies and
see where you can improve those cross-agency services
to better use ACC's available
funds?
Well, ACC, as I said earlier, is a big beast.
And I mean beast in a positive way.
It's a big entity and there are some, I think, obvious
efficiency gains to be made by using better technology.
Had an opportunity only a few months ago to go up to
Shamrock House here in Wellington, where that's one of the ACC
call center areas, and listened to calls coming in to the
0800 number. So those people who operate that call
center
are very skilled. They literally don't know what's going to be coming through the
next call. But at the end of each call,
what they used to do was actually have
a few minutes, sometimes five, 10, 15 minutes, to key
in file note comments about the
call, the client, the details, what was agreed, what was
done, and all that sort of stuff. And
given my age- ... I find this stuff still quite miraculous.
Those
phone conversations are now scribed, and at the
end of the conversation, the
AI tool fills in the
notes.
And it's very impressive to see. That saves, at the end of each
call, literally minutes, if not 10 or 15 minutes after each
call.
Which is a huge efficiency gain.
Mm.
And the accuracy sometimes, I would argue, is potentially more accurate
than
a human doing it. It takes, for instance,
the emotion out of a-
Mm
... a note, a file note, and things like that.
It's a factual recording. So that's just one
little example, but I know that there are others as well.
Yeah. And we're being very thoughtful.
We have a lot of data, and there's a lot of reviewing
of data by humans. AI can look at that
data, and the humans can review the AI tool.
So we're being very thoughtful about how we use AI within the organization.
But it certainly does provide the ability to be more
effective and spend more time face-to-face with clients.
Bra.
All right. Next question, primary question from Priyanka Radhakrishna. Thanks.
Minister, I've got a couple of questions on sexual abuse services, service
provision, but also some of the
intervention side of that.
There has been some reporting around
existing gaps when it comes to sexual abuse service
provision that is funded, I guess, through ACC,
and yet the budget's largely unchanged between '25 and '26.
Mm.
Keen to get a sense of what some of those key gaps are.
What are your priorities around
addressing those gaps?
Look, I'm going to let the Associate Minister, as I said
earlier, the delegation for prevention, because I know that
you've got some points you'd like to make in response.
Mm-hmm, sure.
Excuse me.
Look,
this is I think an increasing area of work for the
agency.
Sexual violence prevention is something that everybody's thinking very
deeply about, because we are seeing incidences of reporting, at the very
least, rising, which therefore would correlate potentially
with incidences occurring within the community.
So ACC is very focused on investing in
community-based sexual violence prevention initiatives
that really do rely on community capability to
reduce the drivers of that harm that are occurring.
ACC is one of the partner agencies that are helping to
redesign, redevelop, and drive Te Orehure, the action
plan.
We, I say the royal we, ACC,
is looking to spend about $7 million across the
'25, '26 year on prevention
of sexual violence initiatives, and I suspect the member might be
talking to one particular provider that's been in the media
recently.
Can I make that presumption?
Part of that was going to be the next question, so yeah.
Okay. So look, ACC is, as I
say, focused on
funding preventative type services as well as
rehabilitation. But it's actually investing already
in a range of initiatives which fall under the umbrella program of
Hiketia for our future. There's actually five community
partners across the country. The Minister of Northland will be pleased to
hear there is one in Northland.
Bay of Plenty, Gisborne, Nelson, Marlborough, and Dunedin.
I actually also launched just very recently a program that we're calling
Little Matters,
which is to help families talk about what is right and what
is wrong because the data's shown us very clearly that too much
emphasis has been placed on the child to understand what harm is and
to report that harm, when actually the adult in the relationship needs
to also be able to have those relationships with that child to
elucidate where harm may be occurring and how to talk through it.
So that's just yet another sort of preventative type-
So, Minister, why was Hiketia cut?
Hiketia wasn't cut
So all the reporting saying that funding to the Hikatea program, which is now
stopped, is not true.
I think you're talking about a program that was called Respected, which-
That was two separate ones from my understanding
... was not funded. Megan, you might be able to talk to the practicalities of it.
Yeah.
But as I understand, Respected wasn't funded as a
Hikatea provider.
Yeah.
Respected was a provider.
Hikatea is ongoing. We're continuing to fund that.
What we did was we hit pause last year
on further rollout across more regions until we actually
understand how it's working, if we need to make any
changes. We didn't want to roll out across the whole country until we've learnt
from these first five regions, but it's absolutely
ongoing.
What are the current wait times for sexual abuse assessment and
treatment?
I don't have the exact wait times, but we know it's a
huge
challenge. We've increased the capacity of providers to
support people who come forward. The average time it takes someone to
come forward to seek treatment is 14 years after the abuse,
and so we don't want people waiting any longer once they've made that
difficult decision to come forward.
So with our new sensitive claims service, we've increased the number of
providers. We've also made some changes so that
the more advanced, more specialized providers are focusing more
on the people who need it. And for people who perhaps need
counseling as opposed to, say, a psychiatrist, they've got a
separate pathway. So we're trying to, I guess, optimize the
capacity we've got as well.
And look, we can come back to you with the specific
wait times at the moment. I just don't have them in here.
Yeah, sure. Wait times and demand trend, if you could come back on that-
... that'd be great.
Great, and we have a supplementary from Lawrence Yunupingu.
Thank you, Chair. I got a couple of subs on this.
Can I just pick up on something you mentioned in terms of Respected and
the fact that Respected funding has been paused.
No, that's not correct. Respected was
never receiving funding.
Funding from that.
They were never a Hikatea
for our future provider.
I see. Thank you. I do want to just check because I think
the issue of prevention of
family and sexual violence is an important one, ACC has a role to play in that.
We've seen some stark figures from the Te Ao Rakirua action plan, for example,
where 92% of the sexual violence homicide were known to
authorities.
I want to check because I'm just going through, Minister, your injuries in New
Zealand, and there wasn't a mention
in there at all of family and sexual violence prevention.
And also I'm looking at the three new priority areas and action plans for
ACC, and not a single word on sexual and
family violence, not a single word on sensitive claims.
So how are you saying what you're
doing in this space when it's not being reflected in your ongoing action plans?
Look, that work is certainly a priority for the organization.
Our new strategy is quite a change in the way
we've presented it. We've presented it around a focus
on timely recovery, putting clients at the center
of their recovery. That covers everything from sort of
serious injury,
less serious injuries, road, work, home,
and
family and sexual violence. So in our strategy, we haven't called that out, but we
certainly have ongoing work in both
continuing to improve our service for sensitive claims,
responding to the significant increase in demand
from the community that we're seeing, as well as this being a
key part of our prevention. It's actually within the efficient
organizational sustainable scheme in terms of the role of
prevention. We describe it as always on.
It's a legislative function. As the minister said at the
beginning,
ultimately the best thing we can do is prevent injuries,
but it's
an always on activity for us.
Yeah, I know. And we've talked about the always on activities a few years ago, and
always on just means that it also can potentially be diluted.
Can I just check, this is more of maybe a nuance.
You mentioned in your priority one, putting clients first.
Mm-hmm.
Would you consider people that you're preventing from being injured in the first
place clients?
Well,
there's probably a
technical legal definition of when someone becomes a client,
but prevention is a big part of what ACC do.
And just
by way of background, the prevention
programs are many and varied. They're not just on domestic
or sexual violence or what we would call sensitive claims areas.
It's a very wide area of prevention.
And by and large, I think ACC does a very good job on
that work. Could we do better? Could the scheme do better?
Probably, yes, but that would be true, I think, of any government agency or
department in any endeavor that they do.
I'm just looking at some stats that you might be interested in.
ACC received over 14,000 new sensitive claims in
the past year and supported over
40,000 people. So these are quite large numbers.
So
to focus just on one, that's just in sensitive claims.
Mm.
So,
that's a lot of work that's being done by ACC staff and
officials,
and prevention is just part of that overall overarching work.
But part of that,
I agree with you, Minister, and I think that's a really important stat.
But also if we're seeing- Like
I said, the prevention of--
at risk of going into prevention, which is my primary question later-
Ah. Which is also mine, Lawrence
... because I want to focus specifically-
Mm
... specifically on family and sexual violence.
You're absolutely right, Minister, but I think one of the issues we're also seeing
is that that is people who didn't report.
Who didn't make those kind of claims.
We're also seeing people who are not reporting,
and this is my first part of the question is people who are not reporting, and
that's significant. The second part of the question is, as we've seen
previously when we highlight some of the issues when it comes to the social
securities, the ACC bill that we've seen a few months ago, the
challenge over there is that,
particularly when it comes to sensitive claims, it takes a long time for people to
get through the process or potentially challenge it, et cetera.
It's very traumatic. So on one hand, how do you address people who are
not reporting and claiming in the first place?
On the other hand, how do you address the long turnaround time?
Well,
I would
think, actually, that from what I've seen,
once someone does make contact with ACC on
a sensitive claim, some of those cases are handled,
I think, very quickly.
Some
need to be assessed, but that's the same for any
injury within the scheme. There does need to be a
responsible and appropriate assessment
portion to the
acceptance of the case. But Megan, maybe you've got some comments about that.
But as I said earlier,
I've taken it upon myself to spend quite a lot of time
visiting ACC offices and
facilities around the countryside.
I am enormously impressed with the caliber of people
who are working in ACC handling the sensitive
claims. That is incredibly challenging,
difficult
work, often with people who are in very vulnerable
situations. A lot of it has been historic.
And you rightly point out that in many cases it takes years for people to
actually
decide to make contact with ACC, but when they do,
I'm very confident in the professional capability of
the ACC staff members who interact with those
sensitive claim clients.
Mm.
Mm.
If I could add further to that, just when you're talking about trying
to
identify those people who have not reported or are yet
to report.
I mean, this is quite an academic conversation.
You could extrapolate that out to any form of
preventative measure across government, for example,
bowel screening, breast screening, prostate checks.
Absolutely.
I think we as politicians, but indeed the NGO
community and society in itself, these are all
conversations that need to be elevated and continued
because a sufferer of family harm
does not necessarily know that they are suffering until someone
somehow gets to them and points it out, and they
can then have services wrapped around them,
here's hoping. But
I would say the same thing that the
minister's just mentioned about the incredible
professionalism that I too have observed within the ACC client
handlers. And I, for my sins, also
handle some individual client cases that the minister kindly
delegated to me, and a lot of those do fall into
that sensitive claims pool. And I just had actually a man in his 80s
come up to me at field days the other day saying just 20 years ago,
he remembered what had occurred to him as a child, and he said he could not get
over how good ACC was, how brilliant, how fleet of foot they
were, and he said, "And if anyone ever criticizes ACC to you, you
tell them to come to me."
Which is very kind of him to say so, but the-
But this wider point, sorry if I can just interrupt.
This wider point is that responsibility for
prevention of what we collectively refer to as
sensitive case issues is not entirely the responsibility of
ACC. We collectively, as a nation, as citizens,
as parliamentarians, we collectively have a responsibility over
a range of portfolios. There's a
role for education, for justice, for
social development. There's a whole range of government agencies and support
services that play
or have a part of this complicated
mosaic that is what we, as I say, collectively refer to as
sensitive claims.
Right. We'll round this out with a supplementary on your original primary,
Priyanka, and then we'll move on to the next line of questioning.
Yeah. So it was just a supplement on something you said
earlier, Minister. Now I've lost my train of thought.
Oh, we can come back to it.
No, no. It's all good.
Mm.
And my question was around ACC's spend on injury
prevention, which you have mentioned as a priority a few times.
My understanding is that that has reduced over the last couple of years.
Mm-hmm.
I've heard by 50%.
It's very hard to find this information on ACC's own website and
through WPQs. Can the minister confirm whether
ACC's spend on injury prevention has reduced, whether it is by
50%, and what's been cut?
I'm going to ask Megan, the Chief Executive, to answer that question because I
don't have that information at my fingertips.
Yeah.
Can I just check, do you mean
our total spend on injury prevention?
So it certainly hasn't been cut by 50%.
We set a budget each year when we're doing our internal budgeting.
It fits within our operating budget, actually, the spend on
injury prevention.
And in the last few years, we have actually struggled to spend
the whole amount of the budget
allocation, because
as good stewards of the scheme, we need to make sure that we're spending that money
on interventions that will have an impact on reducing claims and

The Workplace Relations and Safety portfolio plays a
critical role in New Zealand's economy.
It shapes the conditions for how businesses grow, how people work,
and how confident both can be in the system that supports them.
My focus as Minister has been on ensuring these settings work in
practice, not just in theory. That means making sure our
laws are clear, workable, and support the government's goals
for growth.
A key priority for this government is progressing reform
through the Employment Leave Bill, which will replace the Holidays Act.
The current system has become overly complex and difficult to
apply. For employers, this has created significant
compliance challenges. For workers, it has led to
confusion and in some case, incorrect entitlements.
This reform is about fixing what really matters.
It will introduce a simpler, more transparent system that makes it
easier for employers to get calculations right from the outset,
and for workers to clearly understand what they are entitled to.
Wasting resources on complicated procedures doesn't do workers
or business any favor. By improving clarity
and reducing errors, we will build greater trust in the system and
deliver more consistent outcomes for everyone.
Importantly, this work also supports a broader objective,
giving businesses the confidence to focus on growing and creating
opportunities.
When businesses have that confidence, they are better positioned to hire,
invest, and create more and better paid jobs.
I do recognize that change is hard, but trying to keep everyone
happy is a recipe for the complexity and unintended consequence
that have bedeviled the status quo.
Alongside this, I'm progressing reform of the health and safety
system to restore clarity and common sense to our
framework. I've heard consistent feedback that duties
are not always well understood, and that too much effort can be
directed towards compliance for its own sake, rather than
towards improving outcomes. My approach is straightforward.
We can maintain strong protections for workers while ensuring
obligations are clear, proportionate, and focused on managing the
risks that cause the most harm.
The Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill strengthens this
focus by clarifying responsibilities and ensuring effort is
directed where it makes the greatest difference, keeping people safe.
It also supports a more consistent and constructive role for the regulator,
so that businesses and workers alike can have confidence in what good looks
like.
Greater certainty in the system will support better decision-making and more
effective risk management across workplaces.
Finally, I've been looking closely at how the employment relations
system operates in practice, particularly in relation
to dispute resolution. Disputes are inevitable,
but the way they are handled has significant consequences.
What I've heard is that the current system can be slow,
costly, and uncertain. That has real impacts on
individuals, on workplace relationships, and on business
confidence. Lengthy and unresolved disputes can discourage
hiring, delay investment decisions, and create unnecessary
strain for everyone involved. That is why I'm focused on
ensuring disputes are resolved as early as possible and without
disproportionate cost. I have sought direct feedback from New
Zealanders on their experiences with the dispute resolution
system, including the role of employment advocates.
This will provide a clear evidence base to inform the next phase
of improvements. Our goal is a system that is
efficient, predictable, and supports constructive outcomes,
giving both employers and employees confidence to move forward.

Okay. Thank you. Next primary question from Willow James Fly.
Uh, good day, Minister. I just want to make some inquiries about the
money that has been reprioritized from an underspend
in Kaupapa Maori and Maori medium education.
It's about $7.3 million per year. $29 million
over the four years.
Why was there an underspend? Where has it
gone? And has it been reinvested? Has it all been reinvested
in Maori medium education in Kuria Kaupapa Maori?
It's on page 130 of the budget.
Yeah, the committee might want perhaps to take some other questions while I have a
look at the estimate document so I can give you a specific answer to that question
if-
Yeah.
... more may come up.
Right.
It's not documented in our reprioritization, so it may work.
Right. So it is, it's on page 130.
Specifically, the title was Kaupapa Maori and Maori medium education
reprioritizing underspend funding,
7.3 million every year for four years.
Page 130.
We'll just get that information-
Yeah
... and we can answer it.
So I just have a question for you while we're waiting for the answer.
Jump the queue.
You talked last time, Minister, about being keen to focus
on including parents in the communication you have about how students are
doing. Have you got funding in this budget for that,
keeping the parents up to date with the students' achievements and things like
that?
There's quite a lot of work that's gone on
and around keeping parents updated, not only through the smart
tool, but through the new reporting requirements as well, and the consistent
progress descriptors that we're using across the curriculum.
Not only there, but also in reporting on schools through the way that
ERO are now
doing their reporting on schools, giving parents much clearer information.
And also through the parent portal, which is new as well, giving parents a lot
more information about what their children are learning, so that they can tag that
into the school report that says,
"Look, we need a bit of extra work in measurement." They can click into the
parent portal on the year level, into measurement, and do a
range of things at home to help support that.
It hasn't been available before. What we're really trying to do is bring parents
far more into the conversation, much better data, and then access to things that
help improve results.
Thank you. So follow me to a question from Lawrence now.
One quick sub. Does the ministry do any sort of tracking on how much time
teachers and schools are now spending on
contacting or in communications with parents compared to 10 or 20 years
ago?
I don't think we tracked it 10 to 20 years ago, so I wouldn't have a
comparison.
But has it been tracked at all?
Not unless schools are sending it through to us.
Schools certainly note that they have regular contact with parents, but we would
expect that
having good connections with parents, families, caregivers is really important.
I think also having to track that kind of data would be overly burdensome on
schools if you're expecting us to try and work out how much contact time teachers
have with parents would probably be something that would just add another layer of
complexity to their job.
Absolutely, but the reason I mention this is because the
more time, it's important for parents to know, I absolutely agree with that.
But it's also important to note that the more time that teachers are spending
having to be in communications or responding to emails, et cetera, from parents,
particularly very difficult parents or difficult parents, what then you will see is
it's time that they cannot spend in front of
a child, or potentially teachers are spending two weekends, quite late in the
evening, having to respond now to parents.
This is not really an estimates question.
Yeah, so-
But this is following on from-
Look, you're welcome to... Lawrence, can we just, in the interest of time, because
we need to come back to Willow-Jean's question with the answer.
Sure. That sounds very good. Thank you.
We can follow up in writing-
That'd be good
... and if it's appropriate, then-
In the interest of, you know
... it'll get passed on.
Thank you.
Just following up on Willow-Jean Prime's questions.
Yeah, thank you.
So this was an initiative from Budget 2025.
It appears in the estimates document because it lists a number of
initiatives from previous budgets going back some years, including
initiatives going back as far as Budget 2022, for example.
The reprioritization was a forecast amount of money
that was put aside by the previous government for the
2023 teacher and principal collective bargaining that
related to Māori medium schools and
the costs of settling the collective agreement in Māori medium schools.
It was reprioritized because the forecast was slightly over relative to
the amount of money that was actually required to pay out on that budget
initiative. And so once we'd redone the forecast in Budget
2025, we were able to take that allowance back.
And so where's that money gone?
Well-
Back into Māori initiatives?
So Budget 2025 reprioritized quite a large amount of money from the
sector and then put it into a series of new initiatives.
I'm afraid we're not able to-
Are they Māori ones?
We're not able to talk about Budget 2025 estimates because we covered that last
year.
So we're very happy to-
But they would be ongoing
... supply a copy of any information from last
year's budget about where the reprioritization has gone to.
Can I just also, I know while we're not supposed to talk about last year's budget,
but it is important that we were the one, I was minister that had a
Māori part of last year's budget that invested
significantly, 14.7 for Te Reo Māori
training for teachers, 10 million to the Virtual Learning Network, which Kura
Kaupapa had asked us for, four and a half million for curriculum resources and Te
Reo Māori, 4.8 for curriculum advisors,
frontline advisors to support kura and Māori medium settings, 2.1 million to
introduce the new Māori subjects.
But what's specific in this budget?
So, well, this was-
Specific Māori funding in this budget
... this was last year's budget, which you asked a part of, and it was
reprioritized, as you've just said, last year.
So I'm just pointing out that there was a huge Māori
education package last year, which is rolling out every single year.
Mm.
And I've always, I mentioned, sorry, you weren't probably here at the very
beginning, where we talked about all of the initiatives in this
budget. As we've done in the past, we always make sure that we are
providing those in Te Reo Māori or for Kura Kaupapa as well.
So that same
equity of funding
will continue through the, for example, the 131 million into the primary
support-
But that's not specific funding
... will be. Well, it is specific because we are providing it in Te Reo Māori as
well for Kura Kaupapa and Māori medium.
So we didn't split it out this year, but it is all in there because we're taking
the same approach of making sure we're equitably funding a bilingual education
system.
Thank you very much, Minister. Thank you to the officials.
We're finished with you and our time now. Yes, amazing.
That 70 minutes has gone by rapidly.
Appreciate you making yourselves available to us.
Thank you all for coming.
Thank you.
We'll see you in a couple of hours.
Yes.
We'll see you ministry officials back.
Yes, we will be back.
Committee
